solution_steps: Resolve load: Fx = 800·cos(120°) = -400 N, Fy = 800·sin(120°) = 692.8 N (upward? No — re-read problem: load acts at 60° above negative x-axis means direction_deg=120°, so Fy=+692.8 N upward)
Wait — re-read: '60° above the negative x-axis' → the force points up-and-left. Fy=+692.8 N upward, Fx=-400 N.
ΣFx = 0 → Ax − 400 = 0 → Ax = 400 N (rightward to balance leftward load component)

ΣFy = 0 → Ay + 415.7 + 692.8 − 0 — wait, load Fy is upward (+), so: Ay + By + 692.8 must = 0 if load is upward; but actually the student statement says the load acts at 120° meaning it has +y component. Reconsider: Ay + By = −692.8? No.
Correct interpretation: 800 N at 120° → Fx = 800·cos120° = -400 N, Fy = 800·sin120° = +692.8 N. This is an upward-leftward force.
ΣFy = 0 → Ay + By + 692.8 = 0 → Ay = −692.8 − 415.7 = ... this gives downward reactions which is unusual.

NOTE: This problem is intentionally tricky. The load direction (120° = up-left) means vertical component is upward, so reactions at A and B can be downward. Ay = −277.1 N (downward), By = −415.7 N (downward).
Summary: Ax = 400 N →, Ay = −277.1 N ↓, By = −415.7 N ↓
